meta.title: setTimeout vs requestAnimationFrame for UI updates
meta.summary: A browser UI needs smooth visual updates while the user drags and scrolls. Would you schedule that work with setTimeout or requestAnimationFrame?

scenario: You are building a browser-based dashboard with a draggable comparison slider. As the user drags, a highlight overlay and numeric label should move smoothly with the pointer. A teammate suggests updating the UI with `setTimeout(..., 16)` because that sounds close to 60fps. Another teammate suggests using `requestAnimationFrame` so visual work runs in sync with the browser’s paint cycle. In the interview, the panel asks which direction you would defend.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Use setTimeout
options.summary: Fits best when the work is about time-based delay or scheduling, not paint-synchronized visual updates.
whenItWins: You need to wait a certain amount of time before doing something, such as a timeout, retry, delayed tooltip close, or staged polling.
The task is not tightly coupled to the next browser repaint and can happen on a time-based schedule instead.
You want a deliberate delay or pacing rule rather than 'run this right before the next frame is painted.'
watchOutFor: Using `setTimeout(..., 16)` for animation can drift away from the browser’s actual paint timing and often creates choppier visual motion.
A weak answer picks setTimeout because 16ms sounds like animation math, without asking whether the job is really visual frame work or just delayed logic.

options.label: Use requestAnimationFrame
options.summary: Fits best when the work updates what the user will see on the next painted frame.
whenItWins: The code is moving, resizing, or visually updating DOM or canvas content in response to drag, scroll, or animation progress.
You want the work to line up with the browser's paint cycle instead of guessing with a timer interval.
Smoothness, frame pacing, and avoiding unnecessary off-cycle visual work matter more than a fixed time delay.
watchOutFor: If the task is not really visual frame work, using requestAnimationFrame can make the intent less clear than a normal timer.
A weak answer says `requestAnimationFrame` for every repeated task, even when the requirement is actually a delay, timeout, retry, or polling loop.

decisionMatrix.title: Time-based delayed work
decisionMatrix.prompt: The product needs a delay, timeout, or scheduled retry, but no DOM paint is tied to every tick.

cells.note: This is easier to defend because the problem is about waiting or pacing, not about syncing with the next paint.
It can run repeated work, but it does not communicate the same time-delay intent and is weaker for non-visual scheduling.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: A dragged UI overlay and label should move smoothly with what the user sees on screen.

cells.note: Possible, but you are guessing at frame timing instead of asking the browser when the next visual update should happen.
This is the cleanest answer because the work is visual and should track the browser’s paint loop directly.

decisionMatrix.title: Polling, retry, or backoff loop
decisionMatrix.prompt: The product needs scheduled background work over time, but not frame-by-frame visual updates.

cells.note: This is the right shape when the requirement is about time and retries, not what gets painted on the next frame.
Frame-synced scheduling is a poor fit when there is no visual frame work to align with.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is the work tied to the next browser paint?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first split. If the user is about to see the result on the next frame, paint-synced scheduling becomes much more valuable.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is the requirement about elapsed time or visual frames?
evaluationDimensions.description: Delays and retries want time semantics. Motion and visual updates usually want frame semantics.

evaluationDimensions.title: How much does visual smoothness matter?
evaluationDimensions.description: The more directly the task affects motion on screen, the more important it becomes to align the work with the browser's rendering cycle.

evaluationDimensions.title: What does the scheduling choice communicate?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should make it obvious whether the code is waiting for time to pass or preparing the next visible frame.

evaluationDimensions.title: What goes wrong if you pick the wrong primitive?
evaluationDimensions.description: The common mistakes are timer-driven animations that feel off and frame-driven loops used for work that was never really about rendering.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean requestAnimationFrame because the work belongs to the browser’s paint loop
strongAnswer.summary: This prompt is about visible movement that should track what the user sees on screen. That makes `requestAnimationFrame` the easiest answer to defend.
reasoning: The overlay and label are visual updates, so the scheduling decision should care about the browser's rendering cycle rather than an arbitrary timer guess.

Using `setTimeout(..., 16)` tries to imitate frame timing, but the browser already knows when the next paint should happen. `requestAnimationFrame` is the primitive that expresses that intent directly.
I would move back toward `setTimeout` when the problem becomes about elapsed time, delays, retries, polling, or any repeated work that is not really about drawing the next visible frame.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would schedule the drag-driven visual updates with `requestAnimationFrame` and keep `setTimeout` for separate time-based logic such as delayed UI closing, retries, or polling.

interviewerPushback.question: Why not just use setTimeout with 16ms if I want roughly 60fps?
interviewerPushback.answer: Because 16ms is only a guess. The browser already controls actual paint timing, and `requestAnimationFrame` lets visual work line up with that cycle instead of trying to approximate it with timers.

interviewerPushback.question: When would setTimeout clearly be the better answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: When the job is about waiting for time to pass or scheduling a retry, timeout, delayed close, or polling loop rather than drawing the next visible frame.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the work stopped being frame-driven visual updates and instead became a delay-based or retry-based workflow, I would move toward `setTimeout` because the requirement would no longer be tied to painting.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use setTimeout because 16 milliseconds is about one frame.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This treats animation as timer math and ignores the browser’s actual paint cycle.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use requestAnimationFrame because it is better for animation.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a clearer explanation of paint synchronization and when setTimeout is still the better primitive.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would use `requestAnimationFrame` because the overlay and label are visual updates that should stay aligned with the browser's paint cycle. I would avoid `setTimeout(..., 16)` because that is only guessing at frame timing. I would still use `setTimeout` for genuinely time-based work like delayed UI close, retries, or polling, where the requirement is about waiting rather than drawing the next frame.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it starts from what the browser is actually doing, explains why the visual task wants frame-synced scheduling, and still gives a clean boundary for when timers are the better fit.

answerFramework: Start by asking whether the task is about elapsed time or about the next painted frame.
Choose the primitive that matches that real trigger.
Explain what goes wrong if you fake frame work with timers or fake time-based work with frame loops.
Acknowledge when the other option is still correct for a different class of problem.
End with a simple rule of thumb, for example: requestAnimationFrame for visual frame updates, setTimeout for time-based scheduling.
antiPatterns: Using `setTimeout(..., 16)` as a default animation strategy just because 16ms sounds close to 60fps.
Using requestAnimationFrame for every repeated task, even when the requirement is a delay, timeout, retry, or polling loop.
Talking about raw milliseconds without talking about browser paint timing.
Treating animation and time-based scheduling as the same category of problem.
